paragraphs.text: Three years after the death of Sarah, “Abraham said to his eldest servant… Put thy hand under my thigh, and I will adjure thee… that thou shalt not take a wife for my son… of the daughters of the Canaanites” (Gen 24:2–3). The servant swears by the covenant of circumcision. For upon that which men had defiled at the very beginning of their existence, before and after the Flood, God placed the sign of the covenant, and to what was held contemptible in the human body He imparted preeminent honor by placing on it the sign of the covenant — so that by this those who gave an oath did swear, and by this those requiring an oath required their witness. The servant swore to his master, “and took… of all the goods… and arose and went to Aram (Mesopotamia), to the city of Nahor” (Gen 24:10). And he stopped by a well, began to pray to God, and asked a sign for himself. And certainly he was gladdened by Rebekah’s coming to the well, but he tried also to learn from what family she was. When he learned that she was the daughter of Bethuel, son of Nahor, he gave thanks to God and entered their house and stayed there. And as soon as it was told them by what oath his master had bound him, and how what he had prayed for at the well had been fulfilled in actual deed, Bethuel and Laban said to him: “The thing has come from the Lord… both thy thing and the thing of thy master; behold, Rebekah is before thee, take her, and go” (Gen 24:50–51). They called the maiden too, to know her consent. And since she had heard of the oath by which Abraham had bound the servant, of the servant’s prayer at the well, and of the sign which he had asked and which had been given to him, she was afraid to say that she would not go — for she knew it was God’s will that she should go. And Rebekah went, and became Isaac’s wife; and from joy over Rebekah, who had come to him three years after his mother’s death, “Isaac was comforted… concerning his mother” (Gen 24:67), for whom he had mourned three years.
